meta.title: Data-Driven Texas
meta.chapterTopics: Sets, trivia gate, full data-driven architecture

overview.body: Time to put it all together! This MASTER week introduces sets — collections that automatically remove duplicates. You’ll replace the coin flip gate with a trivia system, track unique cities and hazards with sets, and produce a comprehensive final report. This is Module 4’s capstone: data from files, dictionaries, sets, and polished output.

mentorQuote.quote: Lists remember everything. Sets remember everything once. If you visited Houston three times, a list says Houston, Houston, Houston. A set just says Houston. That’s not a limitation — it’s a superpower for tracking unique things.

objectives: load_trivia() — Parse trivia.txt into a dict with list values
trivia_gate() — TRUE/FALSE questions replace the coin flip
Sets — Track unique cities visited and unique hazards encountered
sorted() — Display set contents in alphabetical order
Comprehensive report — Route, unique cities, trivia stats, and hazard stats
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 9: set(), .add(), len() on sets, sorted() for display, and dict with list values
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapters 8 & 9
programRequirements: Your program MUST use sets to track unique cities and hazards
Your program MUST use sorted() when displaying set contents
Your program MUST include trivia statistics in the final report
Your program MUST track and display unique hazard types encountered
overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-4-3.py and your function_library.py this week. Start by copying your lab-4-2.py and renaming it.
overview.additionalFilesNote: Submit both lab-4-3.py and your accumulated function_library.py. CodeGrade tests trivia_gate() in isolation using your library, so both files must be present.
overview.saveYourCode: This is the Module 4 capstone. Module 5 starts fresh with classes. Save lab-4-3.py as your personal reference for the data-driven architecture.

timeGuide.strategy: This is the MASTER week — take your time! Start by copying lab-4-2.py and work through each checkpoint carefully. The trivia gate is the most complex new feature.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here’s how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you’re adding. The [REPLACED] tags show what changes from your 4.2 code. The section at the bottom runs after the game loop ends.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- DATA LOADING FUNCTIONS ----
load_city_distances()   reads city_distances.txt -> nested dict
load_city_info()        reads city_info.txt -> dict of dicts
load_hazards()          reads hazards.txt -> list of dicts
load_trivia()           reads trivia.txt -> dict of lists  [NEW]

# ---- GAME FUNCTIONS ----
display_destinations()  shows menu from dict
get_destination_choice()  validates user pick
trivia_gate(dest, trivia, all_dests, distances, current)  TRUE/FALSE question  [NEW]
handle_hazard(hazards)  random hazard + coin flip
travel_to(distance)    D20 travel + hazard checks

# ---- MAIN PROGRAM ----
Load ALL data files (distances, info, hazards, trivia)
Get player name
current_city = 'San Antonio'
unique_cities = set()  [NEW]
unique_hazards = set()  [NEW]

GAME LOOP (each pass is one leg):
|
|-- unique_cities.add(current_city)  [NEW]
|-- Display city info, show destinations
|-- Get choice with .strip().upper()
|-- trivia_gate()  [REPLACED] was coin flip
|
|   TRAVEL LOOP (D20 rolls):
|   |-- Roll D20, move miles
|   |-- 30% chance: handle_hazard() -> unique_hazards.add()  [NEW]
|   |-- Repeat until you arrive
|
|-- Update trip totals + trivia stats  [NEW]
|-- Check outcome: beach / dead end / continue

=== FINAL TRIP REPORT ===  [NEW]
| Route: city -> city -> city (from visited list)
| Unique cities: sorted(unique_cities)
| Unique hazards: sorted(unique_hazards)
| Trivia: correct / total
| Trip stats: miles, turns, hazards

checkpoints.title: Load Trivia
checkpoints.subtitle: Parse trivia.txt and build a dictionary where each city maps to a list of question dicts.

content.quote: This is the big one. Trivia replaces the coin flip, sets handle your unique tracking, and sorted() makes your report look clean. The game loop barely changes — you’re mostly adding smarter pieces around it.

content.body: A function that reads trivia.txt where each city can have multiple trivia questions. Structure: trivia['Houston'] returns a list of question dicts.

This is the most sophisticated data structure you’ve built yet: a dictionary of lists of dictionaries. Real applications use nested structures like this constantly — think of a playlist app where each artist maps to a list of album objects. Mastering this pattern unlocks serious programming power.

items: Copy lab-4-2.py and rename it to lab-4-3.py
Update the top-of-file docstring to describe Lab 4.3
Define load_trivia(filename)
Create an empty dict: trivia = {}
Open the file and loop through lines: use line.strip().split(',')
Extract parts: city = parts[0], question = parts[1], answer = parts[2]
Check if the city key exists: if city not in trivia: trivia[city] = []
Append the question dict: trivia[city].append({'question': question, 'answer': answer})
Close the file and return the trivia dict

references.description: Your Lab 4.1 load_city_distances() pattern applies here — same file-reading loop, but now you’re appending to lists instead of storing single values. Program 9-1 also shows dictionary creation with key-value pairs; your trivia dict adds a twist where each value is a list that can hold multiple items.

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
trivia = load_trivia('trivia.txt')
print(f'Cities with trivia: {len(trivia)}')
print('Houston questions:', len(trivia['Houston']))
print('First Houston Q:', trivia['Houston'][0])

content.body: Verify: Houston has multiple questions, and each is a dict with 'question' and 'answer' keys.

rows: Forgetting to check if the city key exists before appending
KeyError — you can’t append to a list that doesn’t exist yet
Using trivia[city] = question_dict instead of .append()
Each new question overwrites the previous one instead of adding to the list
Splitting on the wrong delimiter
Check trivia.txt — what character separates city, question, and answer?

items: load_trivia() function defined
Returns a dictionary where values are lists
Each city can have multiple questions
Debug print shows correct city count

checkpoints.title: Trivia Gate
checkpoints.subtitle: Replace the coin flip with a TRUE/FALSE question system that returns destination, distance, and result.

content.body: A function that presents a trivia question, accepts TRUE/FALSE input (using .strip().upper()), and returns the final destination, distance, and whether the player got it correct.

items: Define trivia_gate(dest, trivia, all_dests, distances, current)
Print a header: --- TRIVIA TIME! --- and announce the destination
Get a question: if dest has trivia, use random.choice(trivia[dest]); otherwise pick from any city in the dict
Display the question and prompt for an answer
Get input: answer = input('TRUE or FALSE? ').strip().upper()
Normalize the answer: accept T/TRUE as 'TRUE', F/FALSE as 'FALSE'; anything else is invalid
If correct: return (dest, distance, True)
If wrong: pick a random other destination and return (redirect, new_distance, False)

content.body: To get “other” destinations for the redirect, loop through all_dests and build a new list that skips the chosen destination. The pattern is the same one you used in Module 3 — loop, check a condition, and .append() if it passes.

references.description: Your Lab 4.2 .strip().upper() pattern for input cleaning works perfectly here for TRUE/FALSE answers. Accessing trivia[city] gives you a list, then random.choice() picks one question from it.

items: Load trivia data alongside the other data files
After the player chooses a destination, call trivia_gate()
Use the returned values (final_dest, dist, correct) to update game state
Track trivia_asked and trivia_correct counters for the final report
Play the game and verify a trivia question appears after choosing a destination
A correct answer sends you to your chosen city
A wrong answer redirects you to a different city
T, TRUE, t, and true all work as inputs (thanks to .upper())

rows: Comparing to lowercase 'true' instead of 'TRUE'
If you use .upper(), compare to uppercase strings only
Returning just True/False instead of (dest, dist, correct)
main() needs all three values to update game state properly
Forgetting to handle cities with no trivia
Use 'if dest in trivia' before accessing, or use .get() with a fallback city

items: trivia_gate() function defined with correct parameters
Displays a trivia question from loaded data
Accepts T/TRUE/F/FALSE (case-insensitive)
Returns a tuple: (destination, distance, was_correct)
Wrong answer redirects to a different city

checkpoints.title: Add Sets
checkpoints.subtitle: Track unique cities visited and unique hazard types using sets — duplicates vanish automatically.

content.body: Two sets: unique_cities for tracking which cities the player has visited (no repeats), and unique_hazards for tracking which hazard types they’ve encountered.

content.quote: With a list, you’d have to check if city not in visited before adding. With a set? Just .add() it. Duplicates vanish automatically. Less code, fewer bugs — that’s the kind of shortcut I wish I’d known sooner!

items: In main(), create two empty sets using set(): one for unique cities, one for unique hazards
Add the starting city to your unique cities set right after creating it
After each city visit, use .add() to include the destination in your unique cities set
Update handle_hazard() to accept the unique hazards set as a parameter, then add the hazard’s name to it
Update travel_to() to pass the unique hazards set to handle_hazard()

references.description: Shows set creation with set() and the .add() method. Same pattern here — create an empty set, add items as you encounter them.

content.code:
# DEBUG - Add at end of game loop iteration
print(f'Unique cities: {len(unique_cities)}')
print(f'Unique hazards: {len(unique_hazards)}')

content.body: Verify: Visiting Houston twice still shows 1 in unique_cities (not 2).

rows: Creating a set with {} instead of set()
{} creates an empty dictionary, not a set
Using .append() instead of .add()
Sets use .add(), lists use .append() — don’t mix them up
Forgetting to pass unique_hazards to handle_hazard()
The set won’t track hazards if the function can’t access it

items: Two sets created: unique_cities and unique_hazards
Starting city added to unique_cities
Each destination added with .add()
handle_hazard() tracks unique hazard names
Debug shows set counts staying unique (no duplicates)

checkpoints.title: Final Report
checkpoints.subtitle: Build the comprehensive trip report with route, unique sets displayed via sorted(), trivia stats, and totals.

content.body: A polished final report showing: route traveled (with arrows), unique cities visited (sorted alphabetically using sorted()), trivia performance, hazard statistics, and total miles.
Important: Sets are unordered. To display them predictably, wrap them in sorted():

items: Header: "FINAL TRIP REPORT" with driver name and final city
ROUTE: The visited list joined with " -> "
UNIQUE CITIES: Count and sorted list from set
TRIVIA: Asked, Correct, Accuracy percentage
HAZARDS: Faced, Escaped, Unique types count
STATS: Total Miles

content.output:
==================================================
FINAL TRIP REPORT
==================================================
Driver: Alex
Final City: Corpus Christi

ROUTE:
  San Antonio -> Houston -> Houston -> Corpus Christi

UNIQUE CITIES (3):
  - Corpus Christi
  - Houston
  - San Antonio

TRIVIA:
  Asked: 3
  Correct: 2
  Accuracy: 66.7%

HAZARDS:
  Faced: 4
  Escaped: 2
  Unique types: 3

STATS:
  Total Miles: 410

references.description: The sorted() function works on sets, returning a sorted list you can iterate through. Your Lab 3.3 trip report pattern returns here — now with sets for unique tracking and trivia statistics added.

rows: Printing the set directly without sorted()
Output order is unpredictable — CodeGrade expects alphabetical
Dividing by trivia_asked when it might be 0
ZeroDivisionError if the player quits before any trivia fires — check first
Missing report sections
CodeGrade checks for all required sections — match the expected output format

items: Report header with driver name and final city
Route showing all cities visited with arrows
Unique cities displayed alphabetically using sorted()
Trivia stats: asked, correct, accuracy percentage
Hazard stats including unique types count
Total miles displayed

beforeYouSubmit: load_trivia() returns a dict with list values
Final report includes all required sections
All debug/test code has been removed
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2–9 ONLY. This is the capstone — use everything you’ve learned! Sets, dictionaries with list values, try/except, string methods, file I/O, and all functions from Chapters 2–8 are all fair game.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–9 (all techniques from Chapters 2–8 fully permitted; Chapter 9 dictionaries and sets as introduced this module)
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: list comprehensions, classes, inheritance, any Chapter 10+ features

warnings: MASTER Week: Submit to CodeGrade early and often. The trivia gate test uses a controlled trivia.txt — make sure your load_trivia() function parses the file format correctly.
Make sure to upload all FOUR data files with your submission: city_distances.txt, city_info.txt, hazards.txt, and trivia.txt.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: This is the Module 4 capstone. Save lab-4-3.py as a reference. The data-driven architecture, trivia gate pattern, and set-based tracking are patterns you will see again in real projects.

needHelp.chapterResource: Chapter 8 & 9 BookEx Reference Guides — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example; your primary reference for this lab
ifYouGetStuck: Review the Chapter 9 Sets section: set(), .add(), and sorted()
Test load_trivia() first — verify the structure before building trivia_gate()
Remember: sets ignore duplicates automatically — just .add() and let the set handle it
Print intermediate values — debug prints are your friend
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which tests pass or fail
needHelp.discordNote: Post in the course Discord with your checkpoint number, what you expected to happen, and what actually happened. Include the specific error message if you have one.
